items.q: What is the difference between species richness and species evenness?
items.correct: Richness counts how many species exist; evenness measures how balanced their abundances are.
items.random_wrong: Richness is for plants, evenness for animals.
items.why: Students conflate these. A community with 10 species each having 100 individuals has high evenness; one with 10 species but 991 of species A and 1 of each other has low evenness despite identical richness.

items.q: What is the difference between in-situ and ex-situ conservation?
items.correct: In-situ protects species in their natural habitat; ex-situ removes them to controlled settings like zoos or seed banks.
items.misconception: They are alternative names for the same approach.
items.random_wrong: One is for plants, the other for animals.
items.why: Students use the terms interchangeably. Each has trade-offs: in-situ preserves ecosystems; ex-situ provides safety net but loses evolutionary context. Most conservation strategies combine both.

items.q: Why is burning fossil fuels considered to disrupt the carbon cycle?
items.correct: It releases carbon stored for millions of years much faster than the cycle can absorb.
items.misconception: It creates new carbon that did not exist before.
items.why: Students think combustion creates carbon. It RELEASES previously stored carbon - the issue is rate. Geological storage took millions of years; we're releasing it in centuries, overwhelming natural sinks.

items.q: Which condition defines a biodiversity hotspot?
items.correct: ≥1500 endemic plant species and ≥70% primary vegetation loss
items.why: It's ≥1500 endemic plant species and ≥70% primary vegetation loss. “≥500 endemic mammals only” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which index is most sensitive to dominant species?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Simpson's D. “Shannon's H'” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A community has 4 species with abundances 50, 30, 15 and 5. Calculate Simpson's D (2 d.p.).
items.why: The real figure is 0.35. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
